questions.scenario:
A batch renderer runs three tasks at the same time. Task A writes `part-07.ndjson`, task B writes `part-08.ndjson`, and an assembler task reads all parts from the same path:

```text
workspace=/mnt/devpolaris-orders-shared/monthly/2026-05
needsOpenReadRename=true
multipleTasksNeedSameTree=true
```

Which storage shape best matches this need?

options.text: Give each renderer its own EBS volume and synchronize completed files through the application after every write.
Mount one EFS file system on all renderer tasks because they require concurrent POSIX access to the same paths.
EFS, because multiple compute units need a shared Linux filesystem path.
Store source objects in S3 and give each task local scratch space, but keep the shared rename and directory workflow unchanged.

questions.explanation:
EFS is the AWS shape for a shared mounted filesystem across compute units. EBS is normally tied to one EC2 instance and is not a shared workspace for many tasks. S3 may be the final object store, but it is not the same as a POSIX file tree.

Learn more in [Amazon EFS concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/efs/latest/ug/whatisefs.html).

questions.scenario:
AWS says the volume is attached:

```json
{"VolumeId":"vol-0a12b34c56d78e901","State":"in-use","Instance":"i-0123456789abcdef0","Device":"/dev/sdf"}
```

But `findmnt /var/lib/devpolaris-orders-api` returns no output. What should the team investigate next?

options.text: S3 bucket policy for `exports/daily/*` because S3 controls Linux mounts.
RDS restore targets because attached volumes are restored as DB instances.
DynamoDB partition keys because the mount point uses a path string.
Linux mount configuration and whether the filesystem was mounted after attachment or reboot.

questions.explanation:
Attachment and mounting are separate facts. AWS can attach a block device, but Linux still needs the filesystem mounted at the path the app uses. The next investigation belongs in OS mount configuration and startup behavior.

Learn more in [Amazon EBS volumes](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ebs/latest/userguide/ebs-volumes.html).

questions.scenario:
The report worker fails during render:

```text
stage=render path=/var/lib/devpolaris-orders-api/reports/ordexp_9012.tmp
code=ENOSPC message=no space left on device
```

`du` shows 83 GB under the reports scratch directory. What is the best fix direction?

options.text: Safely remove confirmed temporary files, fix cleanup after upload, add monitoring, and resize the volume if needed.
Move all scratch files to `/tmp` even if the job needs state after reboot.
Give the worker `s3:DeleteObject` on the whole bucket because local disk is full.
Add a DynamoDB GSI so report chunks are easier to search.

questions.explanation:
The evidence points to disk space on the mounted worker path. Cleanup and sizing should be handled carefully so only disposable files are removed. Moving important state to `/tmp` may make the next restart lose data.

Learn more in [Amazon EBS volumes](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ebs/latest/userguide/ebs-volumes.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris is replacing a report pipeline that currently writes everything to one EC2 instance. Order records require transactions and support queries, completed PDFs and CSV exports must survive worker replacement, and each renderer needs fast scratch space that no other worker reads. Which storage design best matches those access patterns?

options.text: Keep order records in RDS, completed artifacts in S3, and each renderer's private scratch data on EBS.
Keep order records and completed artifacts in RDS, then use S3 as a mounted POSIX scratch filesystem for each renderer.
Keep all three data types on one EBS volume attached to the first renderer so the pipeline has one storage location.
Keep order records on EFS, completed artifacts on EBS, and renderer scratch data in RDS so every layer is network accessible.

questions.explanation:
RDS fits transactional records and queryable relationships, S3 fits durable completed objects, and EBS fits block storage attached to one EC2 renderer. The tempting shared-filesystem designs ignore that no renderer needs another renderer's scratch data. Replacing relational records with files would also discard the stated transaction and query requirements.

Learn more in [AWS storage services overview](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/aws-overview/storage-services.html).

questions.scenario: A DevPolaris media team is migrating a Linux render farm that uses Lustre-compatible high-throughput scratch storage and reads large source objects from S3. Jobs run across many compute nodes and need parallel filesystem throughput, while the finished artifacts return to S3. Which AWS storage choice best preserves the specialist filesystem behavior?

options.text: One general-purpose EBS volume attached to the scheduler and exported manually to every render node.
Amazon EFS because any shared Linux path provides the same parallel scratch behavior and S3 integration.
S3 mounted as a POSIX filesystem because object keys provide Lustre locking and metadata semantics.
Amazon FSx for Lustre linked to S3, with performance and lifecycle settings designed for the render workload.

questions.explanation:
FSx for Lustre is the specialist managed filesystem aligned with parallel, high-throughput render and analytics workloads and can integrate with S3 data. EFS provides general shared NFS semantics but is not interchangeable with Lustre behavior. A single EBS volume does not provide the required multi-node filesystem, and S3 object naming is not POSIX locking or metadata.

Learn more in [Amazon FSx for Lustre](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/fsx/latest/LustreGuide/what-is.html).

questions.scenario:
The worker logs `ENOSPC` while writing a report. `df -h /var/lib/devpolaris-orders-api` shows:

```text
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/nvme1n1     98G   98G     0 100% /var/lib/devpolaris-orders-api
```

What is the best first conclusion?

options.text: Resize the instance root volume, even though the full filesystem is the separately mounted `/dev/nvme1n1` device.
Delete the report directory immediately without checking retention, open files, or whether the job can regenerate its output.
Increase EBS throughput because an `ENOSPC` error indicates insufficient I/O operations rather than exhausted capacity.
The mounted filesystem is full, so cleanup, volume sizing, and monitoring need review.

questions.explanation:
`ENOSPC` and zero available space on `/dev/nvme1n1` establish a capacity problem on that mounted filesystem. The team should identify retained data, cleanup safety, required growth, and alarms before resizing or deleting files. Root-volume sizing and EBS throughput do not change free space on this separate full mount.

Learn more in [Amazon EBS volumes](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ebs/latest/userguide/ebs-volumes.html).

questions.scenario:
A batch renderer task cannot mount the shared workspace:

```text
workspace=/mnt/devpolaris-orders-shared
mount.nfs4: Connection timed out
filesystem=fs-0abc1234
clientSecurityGroup=sg-renderer
mountTargetSecurityGroup=sg-efs-target
```

What should the team inspect first?

options.text: The EFS mount target network path, especially security group rules for NFS from the renderer tasks.
The POSIX owner and mode on the workspace directory, even though the client times out before mounting it.
The EFS throughput mode, because low throughput prevents the client from establishing the NFS connection.
The application subdirectory name, because a missing directory produces a network connection timeout.

questions.explanation:
A mount connection timeout occurs before POSIX permissions or application subdirectories are evaluated. The client needs DNS and network reachability to an EFS mount target, including NFS TCP 2049 through the mount-target security group. Throughput settings affect I/O after connection, not establishment of the mount.

Learn more in [EFS network access](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/efs/latest/ug/manage-fs-access.html).
